# Results

The top model for Snakeden Run based on $AIC_c$ was $S_(time) p_(year) r_(year)$. 
However, estimates for several $r$ parameters failed to converge.
Similarly, the second model $(S_(time) p_(.) r_(year))$, had similar convergence issues for some $r$ parameters.
Consequently, we selected the third model ($S_(time) p_(.) r_(.)$) for estimating survival and abundance at Snakeden Run.

The top model for Glade Run was ($S_(time) p_(year) r_(year)$).
Some estimates of both $S$ and $r$ parameters failed to converge.
Consequently we selected the second model ($S_(time) p_(year) r_(.)$) for estimating survival and abundance at Glade Run. 

Monthly survival estimates ranged from `{r} min_monthly_survival_snakeden` to `{r} max_monthly_survival_snakeden` for Snakeden Run and from `{r} min_monthly_survival_glade` to `{r} max_monthly_survival_glade` for Glade Run (@tbl-survival).
Interval survival estimates ranged from `{r} min_interval_survival_snakeden` to `{r} max_interval_survival_snakeden` for Snakeden Run and from `{r} min_interval_survival_glade` to `{r} max_interval_survival_glade` for Glade Run (@tbl-survival and @fig-survival).
Trends in survival over time were similar at both sites.
However, Snakeden Run had lower survival than Glade Run during all intervals.

Corresponding abundance estimates ranged from `{r} min_abundance_snakeden` to `{r} max_abundance_snakeden` at Snakeden Run and `{r} min_abundance_glade` to `{r} max_abundance_glade` at Glade Run (@tbl-abundance and @fig-abundance). 
Abundance at Snakeden Run declined more rapidly than Glade Run due to consistently lower interval survival estimates. 

# Discussion

Estimated survival was higher at Glade Run for all occasions.
The comparatively high survival at Glade Run was expected based on stream characteristics and conditions observed over the course of the survey.
Snakeden Run has noticeably greater channel size, discharge magnitude, gradient, and shear stress compared to Glade Run.
We also observed downstream displacement and stranding mortality due to high discharge events at Snakeden Run. 
On the other hand, displacement at Glade Run was minimal and no stranding mortality was observed.  

Abundance estimates seem to match naive estimates of abundance based on the raw number of mussels observed alive at each occasion.
On the last sampling occasion, we observed  `{r} final_live_observation_glade` and `{r} final_live_observation_snakeden` live mussels at Glade Run and Snakeden Run, respectively.
Assuming that survival estimates from the Burnham model are true, this would suggest that we observed approximately `{r} final_detectability_glade`% of mussels at Glade Run and `{r} final_detectability_snakeden`% of mussels at Snakeden Run on the final sampling occasion. 
This level of detectability is somewhat higher than is typical but still within a reasonable range for freshwater mussels (**Reference**).

Based on confirmed mortality alone, the maximum survival over the entire 2.5-year study period is 66% at Snakeden Run and 84% at Glade Run. 
This is higher than the estimates from the mark-recapture analysis (Snakeden: `{r} total_survival_snakeden`% and Glade: `{r} total_survival_glade`%).
This is not surprising since the mark-recapture estimates account for the probabilities of both detecting a live mussel ($p$) and recovering a dead mussel ($r$), in addition to survival. 
Further, it is possible that our survival estimates are slightly underestimated compared to true survival.
Of the mussels observed either alive or dead at least once, `{r} retag_proportion_snakeden`% were untagged at Snakeden Run and `{r} retag_proportion_glade`% at Glade Run, suggesting at least some tag loss.
Individuals with lost tags are modeled as if they were not seen again, which can have the effect of underestimating survival (@nelson.etal_1980_EffectBandLoss, @diefenbach.alt_1998_ModelingEvaluationEar, @touzalin.etal_2023_MarkLossCan).
If our survival estimates are negatively biased, the true abundance on the last occasion would be correspondingly higher and detectability on the last occasion would be more in line with typical detectability of freshwater mussels.

# Figures

![Interval survival estimates from the selected Burnham joint model. Error bars indicate 95% CIs. Note that intervals are unequal. Estimates are the actual survival from one occasion to the next and cannot be directly compared.](../../results/figures/05a_alt_model_results/Figure_1_survival_selected_model.jpg){#fig-survival}

![Estimated abundance on each occasion as calculated using interval survival estimates from the selected Burnham joint model. Error bars indicate 95% CIs. There are no error bars for the Release cohort because it is not an estimate.](../../results/figures/05a_alt_model_results/Figure_2_abundance_selected_model.jpg){#fig-abundance}

# Tables

```{r}
#| label: tbl-occasions
#| tbl-cap: 'Sampling dates for mark-recapture surveys. '
#| ft.keepnext: false

make_occasion_table(tbl_occasion_info)

```



```{r}
#| label: tbl-survival
#| tbl-cap: 'Survival for intervals between sampling occasions as estimated from the selected Burnham joint model.'
#| ft.keepnext: false

make_survival_table(tbl_survival_summary)

```

\newpage
```{r}
#| label: tbl-abundance
#| tbl-cap: 'Abundance estimates of the initial release cohort on each sampling occasion. Based on interval survival estimates from the selected Burnham joint model and initial number of mussels released.'
#| ft.keepnext: false
make_abundance_table(tbl_abundance_summary)
```
